trending.title: EU Accuses Meta of Using WhatsApp AI Fees to Block Rival Assistants — Interim Measures Threatened
trending.body: The European Commission issued a Supplementary Statement of Objections (case AT.41034) to Meta on 15 April 2026, finding that Meta's revised WhatsApp Business API pricing — introduced on 4 March 2026 — is functionally equivalent to the prior ban on third-party AI assistants that took effect from 15 October 2025. The Commission indicated it may impose interim measures under Article 8(1) of Regulation 1/2003 to restore pre-October 2025 access conditions pending a final decision, and has expanded the scope of proceedings to Italy. The case directly implicates platform-economy actors including messaging-dependent businesses and any AI assistant provider seeking distribution via WhatsApp. For Prosus portfolio companies leveraging WhatsApp as a consumer touchpoint (iFood, PayU, OLX), this signals heightened regulatory scrutiny of platform access terms. No fine has been issued yet; this remains a pre-decisional stage.

trending.title: OLX Fined ~€10M by Polish UOKiK for Misleading Algorithmic Consumer Ratings
trending.body: A Polish court upheld a PLN 42 million (approximately €10 million) fine on OLX on 18 April 2026 for misleading consumer ratings — specifically, rigging algorithmic scoring to favour paid listings in violation of both the Digital Services Act and Polish consumer protection law. This is a direct Prosus subsidiary hit and the first significant DSA-adjacent enforcement action confirming that algorithmic transparency obligations apply to classifieds marketplaces. The ruling sets a precedent for similar platforms including dubizzle, Property24 and eMAG operating paid-visibility ranking systems. OLX's parent Prosus will need to assess cross-jurisdiction exposure for identical product features deployed in other markets.

trending.title: Meta Deploys Employee Keystroke & Screen Monitoring for Agentic AI Training — Privacy and Labour Flashpoint
trending.body: Meta began installing its 'Model Capability Initiative' (MCI) tool on US employees' computers on 21 April 2026, capturing mouse movements, keystrokes, clicks and periodic screenshots to train agentic AI models under its 'Agent Transformation Accelerator' programme. The data is explicitly intended to enable autonomous AI agents to replicate human computer-use workflows, marking one of the most extensive internal surveillance programmes by a tech company to date. The initiative sits at the intersection of AI governance, employee data protection, and labour law — with experts noting the practice would be illegal in jurisdictions such as Italy. For Prosus, this is relevant as a precedent-setter for any portfolio company developing AI agents that requires behavioural training data (Ema, BrainLogic AI, Advolve.AI). European DPAs may initiate inquiries given GDPR restrictions on employee monitoring.

trending.title: EU Digital Omnibus Trilogue: AI Act High-Risk Obligations Face Potential 16-Month Delay
trending.body: Active trilogue negotiations on the Digital Omnibus package — with the next political meeting scheduled for 28 April 2026 — are targeting a deal that would push back Annex III high-risk AI obligations from 2 August 2026 to 2 December 2027. Both the Council (13 March 2026) and European Parliament (26 March 2026) have aligned on the delayed dates, meaning the current 2 August 2026 deadline for high-risk AI systems remains law until any amendment is formally adopted. The delay would directly relieve compliance pressure on Prosus portfolio companies deploying AI in employment, essential services, and education contexts (Ema, GoStudent, Eruditus, Brainly, PharmEasy). Foundation model GPAI obligations under the AI Act remain in force from 2 August 2025 regardless of the Omnibus outcome.
trending.source: Mondaq / artificialintelligenceact.eu / EU AI Act Service Desk

trending.title: Anthropic Claude Mythos Unauthorized Access: Supply-Chain Breach of Restricted Cybersecurity AI
trending.body: Unauthorized users gained access to Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview — a restricted cybersecurity AI capable of discovering zero-day vulnerabilities — on the same day of its announcement (7 April 2026), by guessing the model URL via a third-party vendor portal. The breach was confirmed by Anthropic on 21–22 April 2026; as of reporting, no impact to Anthropic's core systems has been found. The incident directly implicates AI governance and cybersecurity supply-chain risk: Mythos was exclusively distributed to 40+ elite security organisations including Amazon, Microsoft, Apple, Google, CrowdStrike, and JPMorganChase. This is a cross-pillar event spanning AI governance, IP/data security, and corporate regulatory liability — and sets a precedent for how regulators may scrutinise access-restricted AI model distribution under AI Act supply-chain obligations.

trending.title: EU DMA: Commission Proposes Google Must Share Search Data with AI Rivals on FRAND Terms
trending.body: On 16 April 2026, the European Commission proposed non-binding measures under the Digital Markets Act requiring Google (Alphabet) to share search engine data — including queries, clicks, views and ranking signals — with third parties and AI chatbots on fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory (FRAND) terms. A public consultation closes 1 May 2026, with a binding decision expected by 27 July 2026. This is a landmark DMA interoperability action that would reshape the competitive dynamics of AI-powered search and directly benefit any search-adjacent AI company seeking access to Google's proprietary data corpus. For Prosus portfolio companies in AI and edtech (Brainly, Stack Overflow, Eruditus) that compete with or integrate Google services, this represents a structural shift in data access conditions.
trending.source: European Commission / Digital Markets Act official site

competition.title: CCI Sets 21 May Final Hearing in Apple App Store Antitrust Case — €38B Penalty Risk
competition.body: India's Competition Commission of India (CCI) scheduled a final hearing for 21 May 2026 after Apple repeatedly refused to submit financial data required to calculate penalties in the longstanding App Store abuse-of-dominance case (originating from a 2021 complaint). CCI investigators concluded in July 2024 that Apple abused its dominant position by mandating its in-app purchase system at commissions up to 30%. Apple has challenged India's penalty framework at the Delhi High Court, arguing that the 2023 Competition Act amendment allowing global-turnover-based penalties is unlawful — with exposure potentially reaching $38 billion. This case mirrors broader global App Store antitrust enforcement and is relevant to Prosus portfolio companies (Meesho, PharmEasy, Rapido, ixigo) dependent on Apple's App Store distribution in India.

competition.title: EU Commission Proposes DMA Measures Requiring Google to Share Search Data with Rivals
competition.body: The European Commission on 16 April 2026 proposed formal interoperability measures under Article 6(11) DMA requiring Google to grant third parties — including AI chatbot providers — access to search query data, click streams, impressions and ranking signals on FRAND terms. The public consultation closes 1 May 2026; a binding decision is expected by 27 July 2026. This follows Google's gatekeeper designation under the DMA and is the first data-access measure proposed specifically to benefit AI competitors of Google Search. For Prosus companies operating in AI-assisted search, edtech or content platforms, this could open data access pathways previously unavailable.

competition.title: EC Supplementary Objections to Meta: WhatsApp Business API Pricing Violates Antitrust Rules
competition.body: The European Commission issued Supplementary Statement of Objections to Meta on 15 April 2026 in case AT.41034, finding that revised WhatsApp Business API access fees introduced on 4 March 2026 constitute a de-facto continuation of the prior ban on third-party AI assistants. The Commission intends to apply interim measures under Article 8(1) Regulation 1/2003 to restore access conditions to pre-October 2025 levels. Proceedings have been expanded to cover Italy. The investigation threatens Meta's ability to monetise WhatsApp Business API access and could require structural changes to WhatsApp's API pricing model across the EU.

competition.title: CMA Issues First Financial Penalty Under New Consumer Powers: £4.2M Drip Pricing Fine on AA
competition.body: The UK Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) fined the AA £4.2 million on 15–17 April 2026 for drip pricing — concealing mandatory fees in online booking flows — marking the first financial penalty under the CMA's new direct consumer enforcement powers introduced under the Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act (DMCCA). The CMA also ordered £760,000 in refunds to more than 80,000 customers. This sets a binding precedent for all UK-facing ecommerce and platform businesses that use staged pricing or add-on fee structures. Prosus portfolio companies operating in the UK (OLX, Sameday, Just Eat Takeaway) should review their online checkout flows for compliance with this new enforcement posture.
competition.source: Wilson Sonsini / Pinsent Masons / CMA Blog

competition.title: EU General Court Clarifies Antitrust Inspection Cost Rules in Red Bull Case
competition.body: On 22 April 2026, the EU General Court dismissed Red Bull's annulment action against an EC decision refusing reimbursement of legal costs incurred during an antitrust on-site inspection continuation, clarifying under the Nexans precedent that only costs arising exclusively from continuation of a Commission inspection at its own premises are reimbursable. The ruling tightens the procedural costs framework for companies subject to EU dawn raids. Relevant to any Prosus entity that may face Commission inspections in ongoing or future competition investigations.

competition.title: EDPB Announces Stakeholder Event on Competition and Data Protection Intersection
competition.body: The European Data Protection Board announced on 23 April 2026 a forthcoming stakeholder event on the intersection of competition law and data protection — signalling a growing regulatory convergence between DPA and NCA mandates. While procedural in nature, the event reflects accelerating co-ordination between GDPR enforcement and DMA/competition proceedings, particularly in the context of data-access remedies and platform self-preferencing. This is directly relevant to Prosus portfolio companies that process large datasets and could face combined regulatory scrutiny from both competition and data protection authorities simultaneously.

privacy.title: Italian Garante Fines Poste Italiane and Postepay €12.5M for Unlawful App Data Processing
privacy.body: Italy's Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali announced on 20 April 2026 fines totalling €12.5 million against Poste Italiane (€6.624M) and Postepay (€5.877M) for unlawful processing of personal data of millions of users through their BancoPosta and PostePay apps. The violations included excessively invasive anti-fraud measures beyond PSD2 requirements, inadequate user information, absence of a DPIA, and deficient data retention policies. The investigation was triggered by 140 reports and 12 complaints. Poste Italiane has rejected the charges and plans to appeal to the Court of Rome. This enforcement action is relevant to PayU, which operates payment processing services subject to equivalent PSD2 and GDPR obligations across the EU.
privacy.source: Il Sole 24 Ore / ANSA / Garante Privacy

privacy.title: French CNIL Fines France Travail €5M for 2024 Data Breach Security Failures
privacy.body: France's CNIL fined France Travail (formerly Pôle Emploi) €5 million approximately 13 April 2026 for GDPR violations arising from a 2024 data breach that exposed the personal data of millions of job seekers including names, addresses, social security numbers, and employment histories. Violations covered inadequate security measures (Article 32), excessive data retention (Articles 5(1)(c) and (e)), and delayed breach notification (Article 33). This is part of CNIL's 2026 public sector enforcement push and signals that large-scale data processors — including those in gig economy and workforce platforms — face heightened scrutiny. Relevant to Prosus workforce and platform companies including Urban Company, Rapido, and ElasticRun operating large employee/contractor databases.

privacy.title: Irish High Court Rules on LinkedIn DPC Appeal: Infringement Findings Not Challengeable Separately from Fine
privacy.body: On 21 April 2026, the Irish High Court determined preliminary issues in LinkedIn's appeal of the DPC's 2024 decision (€310M fine for unlawful behavioural data processing), ruling that Section 142 appeals are limited to contesting the fine amount and cannot challenge the underlying infringement finding. The court also confirmed discretion to admit new evidence in Section 150(5) appeals. The underlying complaint was filed by La Quadrature du Net on behalf of 8,540 LinkedIn users, with the DPC finding infringements of Articles 5(1)(a), 6(1), 13 and 14 GDPR. This narrows the scope of judicial review in GDPR enforcement appeals in Ireland — with major implications for Meta, Google, TikTok and other DPC-supervised entities facing large fines.

privacy.title: South Korea PIPC Finalises Revised Pseudonymised Data Guidelines for AI Development
privacy.body: South Korea's Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC) finalised revised guidelines on pseudonymised information processing on 31 March–22 April 2026, explicitly enabling flexible use of pseudonymised data for AI model training and development. The guidelines adopt a risk-based, contextual approach to pseudonymisation, allowing AI development use without consent under statistical, research and public-interest exceptions. A July 2025 Supreme Court ruling already narrowed data subjects' suspension-of-processing rights over pseudonymised data. This is directly relevant to Prosus AI portfolio companies operating in or expanding to South Korea (Tencent-linked entities, Bibit) and sets a positive precedent for privacy-compliant AI training data frameworks.

privacy.title: US House Republicans Introduce SECURE Data Act: Federal Privacy Bill to Preempt State Laws
privacy.body: US House Republicans introduced the SECURE Data Act on 22 April 2026, proposing a comprehensive federal consumer privacy framework that would preempt state-level privacy laws in at least 20 states. The bill grants consumers rights to access, correct and delete personal data, opt out of data sales and targeted advertising (with a significant exception for pseudonymous identifier-based targeting), and requires controllers to minimise data collection to what is 'adequate and relevant.' The FTC would oversee data brokers, who must register annually; no private right of action is included. Critics highlight the advertising industry-friendly carve-outs and lack of meaningful protection for children. Relevant to all Prosus and portfolio companies with US operations including Republic, Remitly, DoorDash, and Stack Overflow.
privacy.source: CyberScoop / The Hill / Hunton Privacy Blog

privacy.title: Dresden Court Finds Meta Liable for €1,500 GDPR Damages Over Instagram Tracking via Meta Pixel
privacy.body: Germany's Oberlandesgericht Dresden confirmed on 13 April 2026 (reported 19 April 2026) a judgment awarding €1,500 in non-material damages to an Instagram user under GDPR Article 82, finding that Meta processed personal data — including name, email, phone, date of birth, gender and location — via the Meta Business Tools (Conversions API, Meta Pixel) on third-party websites without valid legal basis, treating Meta as joint or sole controller. Loss of control over personal data was treated as sufficient harm. The ruling builds on a first-instance Landgericht Dresden decision (March 2025) and a February 2026 precedent, establishing a replicable litigation template. Relevant to any Prosus company using Meta's advertising infrastructure on their platforms.

privacy.title: EDPB 2025 Annual Report: €1.15B in GDPR Fines Across EU During 2025
privacy.body: The European Data Protection Board published its 2025 Annual Report on 9 April 2026, revealing that EU DPAs imposed €1.15 billion in GDPR fines during 2025 — with the Irish DPC alone accounting for approximately 80% of the total value through major decisions against TikTok (€530M), LinkedIn (€310M) and Meta (€251M). The report highlights a continued enforcement focus on cross-border data transfers, AI training data, and children's data. For Prosus, the magnitude and concentration of enforcement underscores the systemic risk of having multiple portfolio companies with EU user data flows supervised primarily by the DPC under the one-stop-shop mechanism.

ip.title: US Court Denies Databricks Motion to Dismiss AI Training Copyright Suit — Authors' Case Proceeds
ip.body: On 23 April 2026, Judge Charles R. Breyer (N.D. Cal.) denied Databricks' motion to dismiss a proposed class action (Makkai et al v. Databricks, No. 4:24-cv-02653) alleging that Databricks used the authors' books without authorisation to train its MPT and DBRX AI models. The court found sufficient pleaded evidence of unauthorised copying at this stage, allowing the case to proceed after a prior dismissal with leave to amend. Plaintiffs include authors Stewart O'Nan, Jason Reynolds, and Rebecca Makkai. This ruling is highly relevant to Prosus portfolio companies that train AI models on text data — including Stack Overflow, Brainly, Eruditus and Ema — and signals that US courts are allowing AI training copyright cases to reach discovery.

ip.title: Judge Signals Survival of Authors' Copyright Claims Against Nvidia for LLM Training Data
ip.body: At a hearing on 16 April 2026 (N.D. Cal.), Judge Jon S. Tigar expressed strong scepticism toward Nvidia's motion to dismiss direct and contributory copyright infringement claims brought by authors alleging Nvidia scraped their works from shadow libraries (The Pile, Books3, Anna's Archive) to train NeMo Megatron and other LLMs. The judge signalled he would deny dismissal of direct and contributory claims but may dismiss vicarious liability claims with leave to amend. A formal written order is expected imminently. This further tightens the training-data copyright risk exposure for AI companies and is directly relevant to Prosus AI portfolio companies (BrainLogic AI, Advolve.AI, Deccan AI, Ema) that may rely on large-scale text corpora for model pre-training.

ip.title: EU Commission and EUIPO Partner for Proactive Counterfeit Detection on Marketplaces
ip.body: On 19 April 2026, the European Commission and the EU Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) announced a partnership to integrate the EUIPO trademark database directly into DSA enforcement mechanisms, enabling proactive detection of counterfeit goods on online marketplaces. This initiative enhances the DSA obligation on VLOPs and VLOSEs to identify and remove illegal listings, with a specific focus on trademark-infringing products. For Prosus marketplace companies — OLX, eMAG, Takealot, dubizzle, merXu — this signals imminent obligations to integrate trademark screening into listing workflows, raising both compliance costs and potential liability for failure to act proactively.

ip.title: WIPO Reappoints Daren Tang as Director General for Second Term
ip.body: The World Intellectual Property Organization appointed Daren Tang for a second term as Director General on 21 April 2026. Tang has led WIPO's engagement on AI and IP policy, including ongoing work on the intersection of generative AI, copyright, and patent law. His reappointment signals continuity in WIPO's approach to AI-generated content, training data licensing, and inventorship debates. This is relevant to Prosus portfolio companies with significant content and IP exposure, particularly in AI-assisted creative or educational tools (Brainly, Eruditus, Stack Overflow, CodeKarma).

ai_landscape.title: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.5 — Flagship Model for Agentic Workflows and Enterprise Coding
ai_landscape.body: OpenAI released GPT-5.5 on 23 April 2026 to ChatGPT Plus, Pro, Business, Enterprise users and Codex. The model is positioned explicitly as a step toward autonomous agent capabilities — 'built to understand complex goals, use tools, check its work, and carry more tasks through to completion.' GPT-5.5 offers a 400,000-token context window in Codex and shows particular strength in multi-step reasoning, code debugging and scientific research workflows. The API is priced at $5/$30 per million tokens (input/output) for gpt-5.5, and $30/$180 for gpt-5.5-pro. OpenAI also introduced cyber-permissive access tiers for verified security researchers. For Prosus AI portfolio companies (Ema, Clarity, Brainfish, CodeKarma), GPT-5.5 expands the capability surface of underlying model infrastructure available for agent product development.

ai_landscape.title: Google Signs Multi-Billion-Dollar Cloud Deal with Thinking Machines Lab (Mira Murati) — Agentic AI Infrastructure Race
ai_landscape.body: Google Cloud announced a single-digit billion-dollar agreement with Thinking Machines Lab on 22 April 2026, providing the Mira Murati-led AI startup with access to GB300 NVL72 GPU infrastructure — delivering 2x training and serving speed improvements — to support reinforcement learning workloads underpinning its Tinker frontier model product. This is Thinking Machines' first cloud partnership and signals Google's strategy to lock in frontier AI labs early. Concurrently, Google CEO Sundar Pichai announced planned 2026 capex of $175–185 billion targeting agentic AI infrastructure. The deal validates reinforcement learning as the dominant training paradigm for agentic AI and has direct implications for how Prosus's AI portfolio companies (Ema, BrainLogic AI, Advolve.AI) should assess compute infrastructure strategy.

ai_landscape.title: Anthropic and NEC Collaborate to Build Japan's Largest AI Engineering Workforce
ai_landscape.body: Anthropic announced on 24 April 2026 a partnership with NEC Corporation to build what they describe as Japan's largest AI engineering workforce, focused on deploying Claude-based AI systems across NEC's enterprise client base in Japan. This marks Anthropic's most significant Asia-Pacific expansion to date and signals accelerating enterprise AI adoption in Japan. The collaboration is relevant to Prosus portfolio companies operating in or eyeing the Japanese market and to the broader competitive landscape for enterprise AI services against which Ema, Clarity, and other Prosus AI-product companies compete.

ai_landscape.title: EU AI Office Launches €63.2M Digital Europe Funding for AI in Health, Skills and Online Safety
ai_landscape.body: The European Commission published seven Digital Europe Programme funding calls totalling €63.2 million on 21 April 2026, targeting AI in healthcare (€9M for medical image screening, €24M for digital health services under the European Health Data Space), digital skills (€12.5M), and online safety (including children's internet safety centres). The calls close 1 October 2026. The funding aligns with the Commission's AI Continent Action Plan and Apply AI Strategy. Portfolio companies in health AI (Corti, PharmEasy) and edtech (Eruditus, GoStudent, Brainly) should assess eligibility for Digital Europe grants, which carry no GPAI model obligations but strengthen EU-aligned AI deployment positioning.

ai_landscape.title: EU AI Act: Ireland Issues First Article 11 Documentation Request — SaaS Candidate Screening System
ai_landscape.body: In the week of 4 April 2026, Ireland became the first EU member state to issue a formal Article 11 (technical documentation) request under the EU AI Act, targeting a SaaS candidate-screening system operating in the high-risk employment category under Annex III. This represents the first concrete enforcement signal ahead of the 2 August 2026 deadline for Annex III obligations (or 2 December 2027 if the Digital Omnibus delay is adopted). Ireland's role as lead market surveillance authority for many EU-headquartered tech companies means this early activity is highly relevant to Prosus and portfolio companies deploying AI in HR, recruitment, or employment contexts (Ema, Urban Company, Rapido).
ai_landscape.source: EU AI Act Enforcement Tracker / euaiactnyc.com

ai_landscape.title: Anthropic Introduces Claude Design via Anthropic Labs — New Generative Design AI Product
ai_landscape.body: Anthropic launched Claude Design on 17 April 2026 via its Anthropic Labs experimental product line, introducing a generative AI tool for design workflows. The product expands Anthropic's consumer and enterprise product surface beyond text-based Claude interactions into multimodal creative applications. Claude Design is relevant to competitive positioning of Prosus portfolio companies in creative AI and design-adjacent services, and may create new AI-training data questions around design IP and TDM exemptions under applicable copyright frameworks.

ai_landscape.title: Google Repositions Jules from Coding Agent to End-to-End Agentic Product Development Platform
ai_landscape.body: At Google Cloud Next '26 (22 April 2026), Google upgraded its Jules coding agent to an 'end-to-end agentic product development platform' capable of reading full product context, autonomously determining next development steps, and submitting pull requests — opening a waitlist for the new version. Google CEO Sundar Pichai simultaneously announced $175–185 billion in 2026 capex to support agentic AI infrastructure. This product repositioning signals that agentic software engineering is rapidly maturing from experiment to enterprise production, with implications for AI-assisted development companies including CodeKarma and watchTowr in the Prosus portfolio.

ai_landscape.title: UK CMA and DRCF Issue Consumer and Competition Law Guidance Applicable to AI Agents
ai_landscape.body: The UK CMA and Digital Regulation Cooperation Forum (DRCF) issued joint guidance in March 2026 (reported and referenced in enforcement context through April 2026) applying existing consumer protection and competition law to AI agent behaviour. The guidance clarifies that AI agents acting on behalf of consumers or businesses are subject to existing UK consumer law and competition rules without the need for new legislation. This is the first regulator-issued framework in the UK explicitly addressing agentic AI conduct, and is directly relevant to Prosus portfolio companies deploying consumer-facing agents (Ema, Clarity, Brainfish, OLX AI features).
